review: The Members Only brand was created by Europe Craft Imports in 1975 and introduced to the American market in 1979.  In 1981, the Members Only jacket was introduced to the market, and thus, a legend was born.  You can more or less take anything else that ever had the Members Only label, and throw that out the window.When people mention Members Only, they are only talking about the racing style jacket.  A reasonable, non leather, facsimile of a "cafe racer" ( Schott Leather Fitted Cafe Racer 660 Black ) type motorcycle jacket.  Light weight.  Two hand warmer pockets.  One left breast pocket with the Members Only logo prominently displayed.  Epaulettes for the military look.  Band collar with a double snap throat latch to keep the wind out.  Polyester.  Tight fitting.  The first piece of iconic fashion item that would later define the 80's.In the 1980's, everybody who was anybody had one of these.  They were the coveted item, and when you walked down the halls of any high school, all that you saw were a sea of Members Only.  Everyone, guys, girls, guidance counselors, and teachers wore them.  I can remember people who would commute to work with their Members Only jackets on over their slacks, shirt, and tie.  If you had a pair of Raybans, a Members Only jacket, an Izod shirt, and a pair of Guess jeans, no barfly could keep her hands off of you.Members Only jackets were readily identifiable by the white label on the breast pocket.  The Members Only logo may very well be the first of brand logos that would come to define the brand conscious consumers of the decade; whose idea of fashion was to wear large block advertising in order to impress others with the prestige associated with those brands.  In the decades prior, 40's, 50's, 60's, 70's, nobody wore clothing with huge logos like FILA, GUESS, ESPRIT, NIKE, POLO, CK, ADIDAS, splashed across the front like some kind of Madison Avenue graffiti.  The idea was to show everyone else how much money you were willing to spend on status associated with a brand's logo.  Manufacturers' labels were once restricted to only the inside lining.  This also sparked the market for counterfeit logo clothing sold in places like Chinatown and flea markets.This is a replicate.  Just like Derby of San Francisco, the original brand has gone out of business, and has been sold and licensed several different times.  What you will get is a copy of the iconic 80's jacket, made from lesser quality material.  True collectors will hunt thrift stores for the real deal.  If you buy this, you're getting something that is equivalent to a cheap knock-off that the kids wearing real Members Only would have made fun of.  But what choice do you have?  It's not like you're really going to be able to find an original jacket in mint condition.  Do you really want to wear something that is 30 years old?

review: Automatic and mechanical watches need to be somewhat babied, you really shouldn't be wearing one when golfing, hammering, chopping wood or other similar activities. At a minimum you will probably screw up the accuracy at the worst it could break the movement inside. High caliber watches including Swiss automatics tend to be more susceptible to these things, the lower calibers tend to be more durable. A quartz, battery operated watch or digital watch are more suited for those activities.The watch has a Sellita SW200 Swiss high caliber movement which is almost an exact duplicate of the ETA 2824, as a matter of fact Sellita used to handle some work for ETA.  High caliber automatics tend to be more accurate then the low caliber automatics and have a smoother running second hand.The watch is a little smaller than I prefer now days but for many this is still the size they prefer.  Great classic style that is styled after the Rolex Submariner.  Hands down you will be hard pressed to find a more affordable high caliber Swiss automatic, I can't.  Honestly I am surprised that you can still get this watch for under $300.ETA, the preeminent maker of Swiss automatics is gradually limiting the amount of movements they supply to outside brands and holding them for their Swatch Group in house brands and this includes Sellita in a different way, they still get some parts from ETA.  Don't wait, get this watch now because it won't be long before you will not be able to get this watch for under $400, if at all.  Highly recommended.
summary: Don't hammer, golf or chop wood wearing an automatic or mechanical watch

review: This shirt defines frustrating. It looks good, happened to fit me rather well, and feels wonderful, having truly combined the comfort of cotton with the wicking, venting and drying qualities of a synthetic. In fact, even after several cycles of ride/sweat <--> stop/dry while on my bike, the fabric still felt fresh against my skin. The shirt did not acquire the sticky/crusty cling that even synthetics demonstrate after being soaked and then dried by wind over the course of a long bike ride.So why the less than stellar rating? First, when washed, the blue color drained out of this like a blueberry ice pop was melting within. But, more importantly, after the first ride, my backpack's shoulder straps began fraying the shiny surface of the threads stitched along the seams. Let me be precise: the thread's core appears to be holding on strongly, but its "glittery" sheath was coming undone, making the shirt look worn/cheap after one use.One other note. The shirt's cut does not have an exaggerated, long tail. But, the overall length (which was evenly cut, front to back) was adequate to keep my backside covered, even after several mounts and dismounts from the bike.If they'd have used a better thread, and coloring process, this would have been a real winner.
summary: Poor Performance - From an Otherwise Incredible Cotton Blend

Finally, a lot of Casio watches at this price will have alarms and other useful features but not this one. review: I recently mosied into a locally owned shoe store that specializes in custom fitting because I do HIIT training and workout daily and just couldn't find a shoe that didn't hurt my feet in some way, shape, or form. The very first pair the owner suggested was these when I explained my predicament and new-ish foot pain. He measured my feet and low and behold... I was wearing the wrong size shoes. Turns out I need a size SKI aka 12 2A. I took these guys home and immediately put them to use (Indoors only of course). My arch pain was almost immediately gone! However, they stunk for my workouts because they are just a bit to deep in the toe box for me and the ball of my foot moved within the shoe when pivoting and twisting and I personally didn't care for that. But as a runner (what they are built for) and for walking they are amazing and SUPER cushy. If I had not needed a shoe for working out (haahaa except now I need shoes for everything since ALL my shoes are at LEAST two sizes to small :P) I would have kept them. I do have SKINNY feet according to my shoe guy, so the problem I had with depth may not be an issue for you. So they are at the very least worth a definite shot if you have arch/heel pain related to plantar fasciitis!
summary: Great shoe for Plantar Fascia issues.

review: To start, I have NEVER had any problems with Skechers until now.  Do not get these shoes!I am a nurse who works over 12 hour shifts, spending majority of that time on my feet.  I have always relied on Skechers to keep me at my best -hurting feet make for are hurting body and Skechers have always been the best!  Until now...I received my clogs last week while on vacation (regularly wear size 10, ordered size 9 due to reviews and they fit perfect!) The minute I put these on I couldn't take them off, so I wore them around the house all day.  They were incredibly comfortable with great support and soft leather.  I was so excited to wear them to work yesterday, but within 2 hours of having them on things began going horribly wrong! The tops of my feet began hurting so bad I found myself limping to relieve the pressure and by the end of the day I was literally in tears - I guess this was causes by the so called "padded" collar.  Every single part of my feet seriously ached when I got home.  In conclusion, after FINALLY taking these off last night, the shoe itself appeared to already be falling apart, especially with the stitching trim around the toe unraveling completely.  WHAT???  And my feet are still completely sore with now black bruised knots on the top of both feet.  I will be returning to my Skecher Go Walks for work tomorrow and pray my residual bruising and pain won't affect my work performance.  I hope with time I can gradually get my clogs to wear right, but am literally afraid to even give them another try..... :(
summary: Skechers has left me completely disappointed for the first time ever!!!!!!

review: So this sock is a little expensive, which is why I deducted 1 *, I would love to be able to buy a 3 pack in the $20-25 range. That said, I love the sock. It is just enough extra padding that, IMO, has really helped my comfort level in completing my long runs in my half marathon training. I run 90% of my runs in Vibram Bilika LS size 46, and didn't have any problems until I started getting to 10 miles and beyond. The problems weren't significant, it was just the overall soreness of my feet was back that reminded me of my first runs in the Vibrams. I also sweat a lot, so I think the moisture was leading to rubs on my feet. This sock has led to a dryer run and a noticeably more comfortable one, while still feeling essentially barefoot.The fit is as expected, I wear a Large and while the toes look tiny they stretch comfortably over my rather large toes and then fit in my shoes snuggly. One thing I'm concerned about is durability. I could see my toes sliding in the wrong slot and easily tearing this sock a part if I'm in a rush one morning and don't take the extra second to ensure the proper lineup. I also can't speak to how many runs they'll last. But after two runs totaling around 18 miles I can say I'm very pleased with what I've had so far. If they are able to last then maybe the $10 price tag will be worth it. WIll update when/if they bite the dust.P.S. this is a copy and paste from my Performance Lightweight No-Show sock review. I think I like that sock better, but they are really tough to differentiate from. I guess it really boils down to I like the look (or lack thereof) of that sock better.

review: Let me preface this by saying they are comfortable and appear to be well made.  They have a very sturdy feel to them and provide excellent support and cushioning.So, why did I deduct two stars?  First, while I said the fit was somewhat small, this is not strictly accurate.  When they are on, they are comfortable and my toes do not contact the front of the sandal, even walking downhill.  It was only after I got them that I looked at the manufacturer's website and noticed the Fit Tip that said this style tends to run about a 1/2 size small.  That annoyed me because if it was true, they should have relabeled the boxes these sandals come in to reflect the true size. One shouldn't have to go to a website to learn this.  But, remember when I said somewhat small isn't strictly accurate?  What is wrong is the amount of space you have to get your heel into the sandal.  If you have thicker than average ankles (or if your ankles swell), getting the back strap over your heel is a chore.  The back strap needs to be about 1/4 inch longer.  That would make getting your foot into the sandal easy.  Possibly this would be solved by getting 1/2 size larger, but then I'd have a sandal larger than my feet.One last thing.  Considering Chinese pay scales and the cost of materials, these sandals are priced too high.

review: I tried these shoes in a running shoe store but did not buy them.  Instead I ordered 3 pair of Progrid Ride 4 shoes from Amazon (I've only run in them a few times but so far so good).  The reason is that while these are OK in terms of a fit, I don't like the 8mm heel to toe offset that Saucony (and other manufacturers) are using on their new lines of shoes.  The 8mm offset instead of the typical 10 to 13mm (the Ride 4 has the higher offset) means the shoe is flatter when it hits the pavement.  There are some claims that this is better for your body, but when I try on shoes with the lower offsets (and I tried other brands as well) it feels to me that they have just removed cushioning in the heel.  I don't like this fit since I like the extra heel cushioning when I run.  Whether this lower offset is better for your body or if it is a marketing gimmick to save on putting more cushioning in the shoe I don't know (I searched the net and did not find conclusive proof from respected independent sources).  All I know is that the Progrid Ride 4 feels much better to me and I chose to buy them instead.  Suggest you try the lower offset before buying any new running shoes since this is being done by all manufacturers.  I will say Saucony remains the best fitting shoes in my opinion for those who prefer wider shoes with more room in them as opposed to other premium brands who sell snugly fitting shoes.

review: *Update May 28 2013*Well I'm returning the shoes. After wearing them everyday since I got them I have found a few fatal flaws. The main reason that I chose to return these came about after two solid days of using them on trails in New Mexico. New Mexico is an incredibly hot yet dry place so I didn't think I'd have to be concerned about my feet sweating a bunch as well as both the shoes and my feet stinking like a garbage dump. I've never had a problem with stinky feet and I normally hike in boots and socks in Texas without issue. These shoes just became overly warm during exercise even in the 56 degree temperatures of the Carlsbad Caverns. I don't want to clear a room every time I take my shoes off.The other fatal flaw was that I kept getting a blister even though these shoes fit very well. The heel of the shoe dug into my Achilles tendon and has caused blister after blister. I gave it a pass on the first blister thinking it was just because the shoes were new but getting one time after time begins to drain your confidence in the shoe. Oddly enough I only got the blister on my left foot and never my right which may indicate a problem with just that shoe.In the end I'm sad to see these shoes go back because they looked so nice and performed really well when they weren't causing blisters or overheating. I even considered powders and sprays but these shoes are only a few weeks old and a shoe shouldn't be this much of a hassle.If Merrell comes out with a better version in the future I'll look back into them but for now I'm off to find something else.*End update*I've only worn "regular" shoes and boots since I was a child and while I've thrown in the occasional flip flop I've never tried a "barefoot" shoe. After seeing a pair of the Five Fingers and discounting them as ugly and stupid I then tried them on and thought they were pretty neat but also knew I'd never spend the money for something that looked so weird. Since I liked them and since my old shoes were nearing the point of no return I started looking at these. I hike a lot and I don't mind running so these seemed to be the match made in heaven, at least according to the reviews, and they looked just like my old shoes.I've had the shoes now for about 2 weeks and the first week was weird and painful. I had no idea how these were supposed to fit and they just didn't feel right. Again I'm comparing them to shoes that have more padding then a mattress but still. My toes touched the front and my heel was being strangled in the back. I thought about returning them for a larger size...until I ran in them.Running in them was a whole new world. I can't describe it other than to say I've never been faster and more comfortable in a shoe. The issue of my toes touching became no existent because my toes splayed out and I was naturally curling them a bit anyway to grip the road. They simply are a great running shoe.I've also done extensive walking in these shoes and I can feel muscles I've never felt before even after great stretching. The walking however prompted a blister on my heel because my feet simply were not adjusted to the shoe. The heel is rather narrow and I was getting a blister in a spot that everyone has experienced and knows is not comfortable. I knew this was going to happen but it meant I couldn't wear the shoes for a day or two.I have yet to hike in them but that is coming and I'm looking forward to it because you can really feel the terrain and get a better grip. From my "testing" I've noticed that only small pebbles seem to cause any discomfort due to the minimalist design.Having worn them without socks the entire time I can say that it is pretty weird but my feet are enjoying the experience. You can feel the wind blow through the shoe and cool your feet which is sort of like being barefoot.Thanks to these shoes I'm going to keep with the "barefoot" shoes and I'm certainly going to stick with Merrell. The "regular shoe" appearance and the quality construction are what made me buy it and the performance is what made me a fan. It gets 4 stars instead of 5 simply because I haven't worn every shoe out there and it's possible that there is a better shoe but I have my doubts.

review: These fit nothing like my other five pairs of Doc Martins.  And let me tell you, I'm bummed.  I read several of the reviews prior to purchasing; all the negative, as there were so few (at this date,) which includes the three star video, and decided that I'd order them.  I was hesitant because shoe/clothing fit is touchy but since I already own and love Doc Martins I gave these a whirl.  They are too narrow.  There is no arch support.  I wish there was a measuring chart for these shoes. Maybe Europe's standards regarding width of shoes is different than the USA's?  I even tried going up a size (as there are no half sizes) from what I normally wear in Docs but that didn't help. (I really wanted them to work!)Apart from the fit, I thought the fabric of the shoe was a bit strange. It looks like coated canvas; stiff without the natural bendability of fabric. I wonder if had they fit that would have made them chafe around the ankle.My honest advice is, if possible, try these on before you buy.  I bought them because: trying them on was not an option for me, I own other Docs that fit great, and the design (black/floral) fit my purpose.I just thought of this; I wear Converse hi-tops and love them.  These fit nothing like Converse either. Just in case you are wondering about that comparison.Good luck! Caveat Emptor!
summary: Good Luck! Try Before You Buy!

review: I ordered and wore this for a costume party and received lots of compliments.There is clearly a hefty license being paid for the "Star Wars" label because there is no other reason a brown polyester robe would cost this much. That being said, if you don't have a lot of time on your hands to sew something up, this is a turn-key Jedi outfit. Just get yourself a light saber and something Earth-toned to wear as a shirt and you're good to go.Be aware that a belt or sewing some Velcro on the inside flaps would be a good idea to keep the robe closed. I tried using safety pins, but the material was very heavy and so the drape didn't look good. You could see the pins. Had I more time, I would have done something more discreet to keep the robe closed because it looks better if people can't see your pants and shoes (unless you've got the full get-up underneath).Also, be aware that this robe will drag on the floor or street if you are under 6-feet tall. Even at my height (6' 1"), I cringed a bit as I walked around because I could see the bottom of the robe getting filthy as I walked around and picked up every bit of dust and grime like a walking mop.Lastly, be aware that this robe has no ventilation and doesn't breathe. You will be dying of heat exhaustion by the end of the party.
summary: Looks good, but over-priced and very warm

review: *Disclosure: I was contacted by Newport Boulevard and given a sample of this product specifically for the purpose of reviewing it.*I'm not going to list out the specs for this wallet again just to make this review longer.  You can read the description just as well as you can read this.  Anyhow, as chance would have it, Newport sent along this wallet just as my old one of similar style was reaching the end of it's life.  This is one is a bit lighter on pockets, but I was able to fit everything in ok without having to downsize.  The ID flap holds my DL quite snug, no worries of it falling out.  The leather is soft and seems a little thin.  In the week I've been using it, the leather is already showing imprints of the cards in the pockets, the finish is wearing away in a couple spots, and it appears that some of the stitching may be coming apart.  Note, the wallet lives in my back pocket, so it gets crushed by my behind and flexed a lot.  If you keep your wallet in your front pocket or jacket, your experience may be different.  Also, some of the premature wear may be due to my overstuffing the wallet (more than one card per pocket).  Overall, it's an ok wallet, hence the 3 stars, but I'm not sure how long it's going to last.

I will update this review if any problems should arise.UPDATE 06.18.14:I forgot to update this post for a very long time, but probably a year ago the winder motor simply stopped working. review: New Balance has quite a pedigree, going back to the original running craze of the 70s.  I've tried them from time to time, generally having some trouble with fit.  Although the shoemaker's 'last' is probably long gone, there is still a model foot that these shoes are built for.  They've always run a little on the narrow side for me.  With this 'D' width, I'm getting a better fit than I have before.The shoe is obviously built well, even though it's unfortunately following the garish clown-shoe look of recent years.  This is not an improvement over their older and more-restrained appearance.  Although I rarely run these days, I'm an inveterate walker.  A few miles on these shoes has not convinced me to change over from my Asics.  Lateral stiffness and support just aren't there, and the shoes feel a little squirmy.  Although there's no guidance on the product page, there's a distinct inward push on the front of the foot.  This would indicate that the shoe is more appropriate for over-pronators.  My pronation is pretty neutral and I found myself fighting the shoe with every step.I'll happily wear these shoes as general-purpose sneakers, but the absence of good lateral support keeps me from recommending them to runners or serious walkers.
summary: Good sneaker, but not convincing as a running shoe

review: Being that I am extremely sensitive to most metals and stainless steel is supposed to be hypoallergenic, (and I cannot afford another pair of platinum earrings at the present time) I thought I would give these a try.  Unfortunately, I didn't even get to try them to see.  They are indeed small as others have stated.  The problem was trying to open and close them.  The latch is so tight I fear I would tear my earlobe trying to get it open... but first I would have to get the thing closed again.  I found that you have to be able to see both the POST and the HOLE in which it snaps to guide it in the right place.  It is not possible to do this while it is your ear.  Not possible for me anyway.  The hinge is quite loose and I imagine it will not hold up to many of these forced open and closings.  I also was not especially fond of the finish but would have kept them anyway if not for the other problems.  I would call it a dull silver color.  Not a brushed stainless finish, just plain dull.

review: The retro style Clubmaster sunglasses frames are very similar to the eyeglasses that I wore for several decades, except my glasses frames were full-frame plastic, whereas these have metal frames around the bottom half of the lenses. But they look great, and they appear to be sturdy enough to withstand the kind of abuse that sunglasses are subjected to. The tinting is just right for my taste and isn't too dark for driving. They're not polarized, however, so they may not be the best choice for boating, where reflection off the water can be a problem, but for other times, they're excellent. The nose pieces are rotating rubbery pads that are very comfortable.A note about UV protection: Exposing the eyes to too much of the sun's harmful rays can result in severe eye damage, including cataracts, vision loss, or even cancer. Speaking as someone who had cataract surgery last month, I take that seriously. The American National Standards Institute guidelines state that glasses should block UVB by 99% and UVA radiation by 95%. The Clubmaster sunglasses are certified by the manufacturer to give 100% UV protection.I really like these sunglasses.A product sample was provided by the manufacturer for review purposes.

review: I'm 5'2" and weigh 135, so there's not a ton of stuff around my waist, but I'm 61, and everything just seems softer and fluffier than it used to be, even though I walk and do yoga/pilates & Wii Fit regularly, so I read the reviews and finally decided to give this one a try.  I haven't had on a "girdle" in 30 years, so maybe I've just forgotten how it feels not to be able to breathe comfortably.  I got a medium and after some gyrations and a bit of a learning curve, got it hooked up in front.  It does give a nice curve to my waist, and I immediately measured 1 1/2 inches smaller :), and when I walk, I have better posture.The down side is that I work at my computer, and I cannot sit for very long with this cincher on.  I think part of the problem is that one size doesn't fit all in length.  It pushes my cleavage up where God never meant for it to be, because it's long on me.  A taller girl might have no problem.  And because my waist is shorter, the narrow part extends up over my ribs, and it actually gets painful when I sit, and I find myself panting little short breaths.  Also, there's the issue of where the fluff goes when you squeeze it in.  I was feeling pretty good, looking in the mirror, till I turned and saw the part bubbling over in back.  That just would look scary with a fitted blouse on........Also, the part I had hoped would be flattened, my abdomen, doesn't seem to be affected at all by the cincher. It's still a pouf, with or without it. I wouldn't call it magical.So, I'll probably keep it, but I won't be wearing it daily as I had planned.  I was already planning to lose 5 pounds, and maybe then it will fit more comfortably, and I'll probably wear it to some Holiday gatherings.
summary: Maybe not magical for short people

review: Moving Comfort Jubralee sports bra; sizes 30-40, B-E: allegedly similar to their Fiona model.Unfortunately, I didn’t purchase my two Jubralee bras through Amazon.  I got them as a final sale from the Swim Outlet.Positives: An apparent gathering type of fit, to keep the bust firm and to reduce shoulder and back fatigue.  Wide shoulder straps could reduce localized pressure on the nerves that run from neck through shoulder and arms, hence reducing funny bone effect and/or pain to lower arms and hands caused by bras that have thinner straps.  The wide rib cage band has the hook and eye closures (smaller size bras have three rows of two hook and eye latches, and the larger bras offer the 3X3 combination because that band is wider).  Both the shoulder and rib cage straps are adjustable.Negatives: Although the shoulder straps are adjustable, I found three faults with them: they’re too short, adjustment is in the inside (flushed against your body), and it’s a flimsy looking Velcro.  Another problem: there is no discernible pocket to those B and C cups that I purchased.  I’m a twig of a person (rib cage under the breast is 26” and with the breast, it’s 29”).  So given that a Bali 32C bra fits me loosely, the 30C Jubralee bra should have worked, but it ended up being too tight even with the outermost hook.  I was prepared for the possible short rib cage band, since I had purchased a hook and eye extender.  The extender, however, presented another problem because I thought that the picture display for the bra was of the 3X3 combo (perhaps for the larger bras).  My small bra required an extender that would fit the 2X3 set.  One reviewer complained that unlike the Maia, the Jubralee causes a uni-boob.  Extenders can add to this problem by compromising a bra’s symmetry.  It’s bad enough that the cups are nearly non-existent on the Jubralee.  Anyway, I ended up with crushed boobs and a choked rib cage.I’m glad that manufacturers are finally designing ADJUSTABLE sports bras with wider straps and bands, hence reducing the pressure to a given body part.  And I don’t understand why most regular bras still follow old fashioned engineering.  Besides, no one needs to sport a sexy thin strap bra under a crew neck t-shirt, school uniform, or work suit that we’ll be wearing throughout most of the active day.  We can leave the Victoria’s Secret revelations for the few minutes that they need to be there.  A well designed sports bra at the beach is also welcome.  However, I want that adjustable shoulder strap to be easily accessible on the outside of the bra.  Such a feature would be great at the end of one’s workout or day, making it easy to release the constraining bra and let it all hang out as we step into the shower or munch on a snack in front of the TV.  And, let’s be real: I wasn’t happy with the tight bra, so I can imagine how it must be for a normal or full figured shaped woman.  There are too many bras out there that are uncomfortable.  I wonder if those torture devices are designed by men or masochistic women who don’t know any better.  The hook and eye, adjustable strap sports bra is a step in the right direction, but it still needs a few tweaks.The Panache sports bra has received good reviews, but I haven’t tried it and I think it’s too pricey.  I hope to soon test the less expensive Carnival sports bra.  If the cups and the Velcro straps on the Jubralee weren’t designed as is, I would give it another try, but for now, I’ll say “No, thanks.”
summary: Too tight; Where are the Cups?

review: Bought the first pair of Bristo Birch Dec 2010This is my second pair of Bear Paw's.  I love my other pair too!  But wanted something different.  My first pair was the Tama, and I just love them.  I now have these Bristol ones, and they are nice too.  They don't have as much cushion inside and fur as the Tama but they are super nice.  The boots run a little funny I weare a 9.5 and usually have to get the 10 in most brands when there is no half size, but in the bear paw's I need the 9.  The 10 would be way to big.  One review said that the boots in the Birch color were green.  Mine are not green at all but very much a Birch color.  So a tanish color much like the picture show's.  These boots are narrow in the toe compared to the Tama so I hope they will loosen with wear because I love my Tama so much I hope that I will have another pair of wonderful boots when they wear in!  Hope everyone loves these boots the brand Bear Paw has sold me, I won't get other brands now because these are just so good!These boots never did loosen up.  I tried and tried to wear them, but they were never comfortable like my Tama boots.  So I gave them to a friend of mine.  She has worn them a lot and loves them.I ended up ordering a new pair of these same boots in Chocolate Nov 2013.The Chocolate Is a wonderful Color, it is a dark brown that is just out of this world pretty for boots.I am disappointed that the boots seem to even have less padding then the Birch Bristal boots.  I guess I was spoiled with the Tama comfort quality.  So lets just hope they prove to be warm.  They do fit much better, I ended up ordering the size 10.  They are still narrow in the toes but not so narrow to be uncomfortable.  I am glad that after two years I got the correct size and now my friend and I both have neat boots.I wish the fluffy comfort was across the board with Bear Paws, but I would still buy these again and hope that I will love them.As a side note my Tama boots are not something of the past, the heals are so broken down that they can't be worn any longer, and much of the rabbit fur has rubbed off the top sides of the boots, but I still love them and I would like to replace them. review: After ordering a Tag Heuer Carrera I became hooked on the idea of automatic watches.  I did a quick Google of 'automatic watches under $500' and the Orient 'Black Mako' was listed by the reviewer as "the shockingly well-kept secret".  The CEM65003D model is not the Black (or Blue) Mako, but it is essentially the same watch.  If you look at the descriptions of the Black Mako and this watch, they are identical.  So the key questions would be:  what are you going to use the watch for, and how high are your expectations for the performance (scratch resistance of the crystal et al).  For $93 as of the time of this posting, I don't think you can go wrong.  The site mentioned above lists an Invicta Pro Diver model that is less expensive than this model, so you are not buying the cheapest of the cheap.  However, after a few weeks of ownership I can say that this is an excellent value, a classic style (OK, so Orient is known for emulating other much more expensive designs) and terrific utility.  The blue face is stunning, the bezel adds additional visual interest and the lume is long lasting.  The urethane band is a little different than other watches I own because of the contours near the dial, and it takes a little getting used to, but you can always choose another non-bracelet band to make it your own.  The bezel is a little firm to rotate at the outset but I assume that will ease over time.  I have worked out while wearing this watch, and the crowns are solid when screwed down, so I have no concerns about swimming or athletics with it.  Nicely done, Orient!

review: I bought these as part of a BOGO sale locally. I also have the KomondoSport, the KomondoSport LS, the TrekSport, and the Bikila.My thoughts on these are:Very slipper like as far as uppers & ease of putting on/taking off.Non marking soles with flexibility.I'm not crazy about the rubber "pod" pads on the bottom because I think they make it feel like you are constantly walking on rocks. Who likes that? I understand for durability & traction, they did this, but they just feel odd since these are a barefoot shoe(and thus, you feel everything!).I also feel lots of seams in these as opposed to my others. I notice that in the others, they are lined until you reach the forefoot area, in these, they aren't. This may be why. These also don't have a removable insole, so you feel the seams on the footbed. My Bikilas also don't have a removable insole, but the insole is wider, so the seams aren't under your feet like these are.Size wise, they recommend to one size up from your usual VFF size. I second that. In my others, I am a 41 but in these I needed a 42. Otherwise, the curvature of the sole makes your toes feel too squished into an upward curve.I bought these to wear around my house as slippers, but for comfort these are my least favorite out of the ones I bought. I feel too much "stuff" related to the shoe design than I would like. When you barefoot/minimalist, the focus is on the feet & the earth beneath you, your stride & strike. Not seams & rubber pods.I give them 3 stars because I still think they are decent shoes with some real world uses. But they could be better. In many ways.

review: These shoes are amazing! I was looking for a pair of shoes that would keep my feet warn and hold up in Afghnistan during the winter. This is my third time in the middle east and I wish I had known about these before. I have used the memory foam slippers before but I found that the soles never held up. These are PERFECT! Because of the way the living quarters are set up here, I have to walk about 100 meters to and from the bathroom every time I shower, use the restroom, and conduct personal hygene. With the way the terrain is out here (sharp rocks, dust/dirt, mud when it rains), it is really hard to find warm, comfortable, slip on shoes with soles that are thick and surdy enough that I dont feel sharp rock hurting my feet. On top of that, they dont slip off if I have to run to get out of the rain. I normally wear a size 7.5 in US sizes but I ordered half a size up after reading the reviews. These shoes are amazing and I definitely recommend them to anyone that is in need of comfort around the house. They can take a beating.

Listen, I'm glad I did.  What I did was 1) Picked out my most FAVE thing from her line 2) followed the sizing info provided by the company providing the item 3) took a risk and just bought one. This was scary because all the reviews say this stuff runs SMALL and then TOO BIG. And it's all soooo confusing. What's a girl to do?!? ;-)So I just followed the guidelines given and ordered the small.  It fits perfectly for me...maybe a TAD bit bigger than I would like.  But, I got sooo many compliments on my outfit. People just loved it.I wore it with knit leggings, a white scarf and thigh high heelless boots and people called me a Snowflake Princess.  Ha!  I think the material is a good quality...but again, I don't mind cheap stuff. .  I shop at Forever 21 all the time and  take good care of all my items (DON'T DRY!) and my clothes last forever.  So, on that note...just give it a try!!! It's worth the gamble. I've already ordered two more shirts. Can't wait till they come!!! :-)

review: I've had this bag for about two years.  Admittedly, I am rough on bags.  I use only one at a time.Initially I was very pleased with this bag.  The front flap has two pockets in it, one more shallow than the other.  It holds keys and chapstick well.  It flips up to give access to an open pocket which is perfect for getting to my cell phone quickly.  I've used the side pockets primarily for holding pens.  It's not realistic to put a water bottle in the side pocket intended for this; it's really too small (unless you find an exceptionally small bottle-I did this, but then found it wasn't big enough to be useful.  The interior fits two epipen kits well, by the way, as well as quite a *lot* of other things.  This is a purse you can overstuff.  I ended up putting my cosmetics in the back zippered compartment, since that was the only way I could quickly find them.  However, they show against the back of the bag.Unfortunately, this bag is not durable, which is perhaps to be expected for the price.  The smaller front flap pocket has ripped from the weight of my keys, and now the fraying edge catches in the zipper.  The fabric overall has become fuzzy and "pills"--something I never would have expected from a nylon bag. It looks very worn after two years of daily use.I would recommend this bag for short term use for a vacation.  I would not recommend it for daily use, if you would like something that lasts.  A more durable, similarly useful brand is Baggallini, but it costs roughly double the price.

review: It's a nice looking belt. The quality is questionable. It almost seems like some kind of card-stock backing and then leather glued to the face. I really hope that's not the case, but I guess time will tell. It's a very stiff belt, almost folds rather than curls. I haven't had a new belt in quite a long time so I hope I'm just too accustomed to my well broken in leather belt. This one says 100% genuine leather... We'll see.As for sizing, people should be using common sense. It's a belt, not a pair of shoes. If you're a 38 waist, order at least a 40+ belt. The size of the belt is the last (largest) hole. If you don't want to have to wear it on the largest hole, order something a little larger. (This seems obvious to me... I'm not sure why so many people have problems; on every belt here on Amazon there are sizing complaints, it's a mystery.)

review: This is a good set for the price.  The underwear and shirt don't exactly match as the screen print on the T is of a lesser quality (appearance wise) than on the underroos.  The britches are much bigger than the shirt.  My son is a little broader of shoulder than waist so he wears a 2t bottom and a 3t top to give room for movement and this shirt fit perfect, even a little more snug than say an Oshkosh 3t T-shirt while the underwear themselves were pretty on par for the size.  I ordered the size 4.  That is something you will need to keep in mind when ordering. review: I'm a fan of Invicta watches, having owned three.  All have been of fine quality, the only "knock" on this brand being that most of their models are far too big for my wrist. (Anything over 44 mm is too big--I'm not a large guy.)I owned the coin-edged bezel model of this watch previously, and decided to buy this model as the price as always was great, and the appearance is way above average.  I swim and snorkel (but don't scuba dive), and prefer a 200m watch for these activities.The 8926 is a great combination of a timepiece suitable for outdoor activities as well as business or dress wear.  The Rolex-clone appearance lends itself for wearing at work or with a suit.  The band is solid, but not too heavy.My two criticisms of the 8926 are these:1.  The bezel is virtually impossible to turn.  I don't really care about this, since I don't dive, but I need one of those can grippers or latex gloves to move the thing.  WAY too tight.2.  The fold-over safety clasp, while a great security feature, is extremely difficult to open.  You need pretty long fingernails to flip the thing up.  Maybe a little WD40 spray will alleviate this.Timekeeping is average for an automatic movement watch.  Since I own quite a few watches, and this requires winding daily, it can be a bit of a hassle to reset it, but that's what you get with an automatic.All in all, great watch for the price.  I look forward to years of service from this one as with my previous Invictas.
summary: Nice watch, with a few flaws.

review: These bags look like a good deal for a set of two--a checked and a carry-on. But the set is really two carry-on bags, one 21" carry-on size and one laptop or "boarding bag" carry-on size. So be careful.The "boarding bag" or smaller bag is more like a laptop roller, and the larger bag is a 21" bag that is the standard sized "rollaboard" which most of us own. As you are allowed ONE carry-on bag on board and ONE personal piece (briefcase, handbag or small backpack) you may NOT to be able to carry on both of these bags onboard at the same time.However, if you want a set of lightweight carry-ons of two different sizes--then this may work for you. Nothing wrong with the bags--they are lightweight but sturdy. However, size-wise, may not be what you need. Here are the dimensions:The dimensions for the carry-on-sized piece are 21" x 15" 7.5"The dimensions for the "boarding bag" sized piece are 15" x 14" X 7.5.The set has all-directional spinner wheels, but I find those more of use on big suitcases, especially hard sides. It's nice to have them on these bags, but not essential.The weight of each bag is about 6 lbs, which IS very lightweight; standard 21" bags tend to weigh in around 9lbs so this is helpful for lifting up the carry-on into the overhead.If you take regional air, the smaller carry-on could be useful because some of those overhead compartments are very cramped, or...if you only need to take a change of a few clothes and are using the carry-on for photo equipment, you don't need the larger 21"--so that option is nice to have. The smaller of the two bags could also work for a child (though if they pack their stuffed toys, it could end up being too small. Small stature doesn't equal small amount of stuff!)The smaller piece opens at the top of the front flap so you can remove your laptop for going through airport screening. The smaller sized bag is reported to have only enough room in the front pocket for a smaller laptop or netbook. For a large laptop, it would go in the main compartment. The larger of the two is a standard 21" carry-on.The small bag is shipped inside the larger bag, so you can store them that way also.The smaller piece opens at the top of the front flap so you can remove your laptop for going through airport screening. The larger is, as I said, the standard 21" carry-on or rolling case.Summary: At the sale price, this is about the cost of a 21" carry-on, so you are essentially getting an extra bag for free. But if you are looking at this as a full luggage set (checked bag and carry-on) you really want at least a 24" or 25" and the 21" for typical use. These are nice, rugged bags that don't weigh too much. If you need a very small carry-on as an option, this set is great. If you are looking for a checked-carry-on combination set, this isn't what you want, unless you typically check a 21" bag.
summary: Might be too small to be useful--these are 2 different sized carry-ons

review: I got tired of looking down at my feet and seeing white sneakers so I decided to get something that looked like a boot. I didn't want to have a heel and I wanted a rubber sole with some thickness so I opted for these. The reviews seemed positive enough and I liked the way the shoe looked. I was chomping at the bit to get these shoes delivered.When they finally came and I took them out of the box they were ridiculously stiff. I could not get even the slightest bend out of them. I put them on with some moderately thin socks and my left foot just completely slipped out every time I took a step. I tried the shoes with thicker socks and the problem was lessened a bit but the shoes still felt really loose. The zipper is just for show since the shoe just slips on and off.Also, when I looked down at my feet, the shoe was really long, making my foot look 2 1/2 sizes longer. I placed these shoes sole to sole with a couple different pairs of my shoes including my Dansko clogs and my everyday sneakers and this shoe was a least 3/4 to 1 inch longer than those shoes. At least I wasn't imagining that they were making my foot look bigger.To its credit, my mom and her friend thought the shoe looked great. I also like the way it looks in person but the stiffness, the largeness and the extra length on the shoe dampened the excitement I originally had for this shoe. Even if the shoe could fit properly (I still don't think this shoe is meant to give a snug fit) I don't think I would like the stiffness of the shoe.Hope you have better luck with this shoe.

review: The pictures are accurate.  The pads have large flaps underneath which will likely tear with use.  I sincerely doubt the flaps will stay together or in place, for long.  But I knew that going in, thanks to the reviews.So what I'll do, is attach velcro inside the flap on both sides, to MAKE them stay in place.  Then, when the flap eventually tears, the velcro will still render the pads functional.  Or, one can cover them with cloth, and sew inside a blouse or other article.For these pads are very thin.  I wanted thin, so that's an advantage (hence three stars instead of two).  The 'sliminline' looks natural enough, under a heavy T-shirt.  The 'petite' are a bit bulkier, not quite a shoulder-pad look.They feel good;  they keep your shirt in place, too;  may need to trim the part which extends toward the neck, if your shirt has a wide neck opening.  Not sure they would work with very thin bra straps.  With wide bra straps (i.e., Champion brand bras), they don't move around, even without adding the velcro; but I'm in a humid climate right now.If all else fails, shoulder pads are great for putting on the back of a landline cordless phone, to ease neck crunch.  I already have thicker pads on my phones, for that reason.  (Hint: attach one or more pads to each other via velcro strips, so you can adjust at will for the desired shoulder-hold comfort.)
summary: Meh:  but can be fixed to make better.

review: I was looking specifically for a shoe I could wear while lifting weights. I was wearing typical, thick soled cross-training shoes which were really irritating. I wanted a shoe that was barely there and close to the ground. They also had to give me plenty of stability and grip, since I workout on a pretty slick floor.  I've had these for over 2 months now, use them 3/a week, and absolutely love them. After seeing some reviews on sizing, I ordered a half size up and they're perfect. I normally wear them barefoot, but sometimes wear a thin sock, and they're still fine. I've never had any problems w/discomfort or blisters, they fit like a glove right away. They provide plenty of support for lifting, but don't try to use these to go and do a lot of cardio. Even the tag on the shoe advises you against wearing them for long periods of time to avoid injury. My workouts are usually around 55 minutes, and they've been perfect for me. I highly recommend them.

review: One of the first things I learned about Chinese-made products is that many different brands can be the very same item with the only difference being a different brand label. Be very careful of Chinese-made products that seem too expensive in comparison to similar items you find that are much cheaper. They could be the exact same thing except for the brand so it pays(saves money)to research a bit before you buy. review: When you buy a driving moc,  you are basically buying a slipper.  Light and comfortable for outdoor wear.  This is that.The ad says leather but it feels a bit funny but it is reasonably soft.The lining is almost non existent which isn't a plus. It should stretch out sufficiently to avoid skin damage but, new, it isn't something you want to wear all day.The padding is just adequate.  A bit more padding would make it a lot more comfortable.The toe area binds a bit.  Again, that should loosen with wear but...The heel area isn't the most stable for walking.  Part is the style and not a real knock but I would call it a smooth surface shoe for room or flat surfaces.  Part of that is what you expect from the style but it could have been designed a bit better.I can get by with all the above with the hope a bit of stretching turns it into a comfortable shoe.If you have a hard to fit foot, I'd pass.  I bought it a half size larger than most mocs and am glad I did.  If it does stretch out as I expect,  I'll insert a shoe pad.

review: I like the cotton stretch hi cuts a lot, but sometimes want a thinner, lighter panty, so I tried the lace hi cuts. These ran small and weren't as comfortable.  This time I tried these satin stretch and the microfiber ones. I bought a size larger in both as a few reviews said they run small - since the lace ones did, I figured I'd better.  I'm glad I did.  When I lay the satin ones on top of the smaller sized cotton ones, the satin ones are at least an inch to 2 inches smaller in width - yet are a full size larger!  They are smaller than the lace ones, which already were smaller than they should be, and smaller than the microfiber ones, too.  I'd say they fit about 2 sizes smaller than the cotton ones, about 1 size smaller than the microfiber, and about a 1/2 size smaller than the lace ones.The material is soft to wear and the waist and leg elastic is narrower and thinner than the cotton ones; the microfiber win in that category.  You can still see the panty line more visibly in the satin stretch than in the microfiber ones.  And, while the satin ones do stretch some (they are called satin stretch), they don't stretch much - both the cotton and the microfiber stretch much more.  So, in almost every way, the cotton stretch and the microfiber are both much better.The biggest issue, though, is that I just bought these 2 weeks ago.  I've only worn each pair twice.  The second time I wore one of the pairs, I realized the thread connecting the cotton crotch area was unraveling and there was a small hole! Within a few hours, that hole had spread to more than an inch in size, so I had to remove them.  The seam, I guess, wasn't finished correctly, so it's just unraveling.  I'm glad I was home when it happened.  It could have been embarrassing if that had happened at work.Since I'm much happier with the microfiber ones for a thin, lightweight panty,

review: You guessed it. The Kayano 17 from ASICS is the best running shoe I currently own. And I currently own too many pairs of running shoes. The New Balance 1906 ,1226 and the 993. I also own the ASICS GT 1260 and the Nike Vomero 5 and  the Equalon 4. I have the Brooks Beast and the Trance 10. Afraid to buy Saucony because of the lack of a wide width (2E) option. I have not been an ASICSs fan before. In fact I sent back a pair of ASICS Kinetics because the hell strike was stiff. I had declared the Brooks Trance 10 my favorite of all time but I gave ASICS one more try and I feel fortunate the Kayano 17 was my reward. There is no question in my mind that every time I put on these shoes I want to work out and run. I have been finding many excuses to wear them. Cushion is unmatched. The hell fit and toe fit and forefoot fit and finish are a godsend. I wish I could buy 2. Like I said I already own too many running shoes. That said I broke down and have a pair of the new ASICS Nimbus on the way as this is being written. Will give a report on these later. This shoe is good for heavy runners and was built for the mild to moderate overpronator. I usually buy Neutral + shoes. These still balance out nicely. ASICS built a shoe that virtually anyone would be happy with. This is extremely difficult to do given the various foot types which exists. These shoes are not mushy and energy return is excellent. I run about 21 to 25 miles weekly and this is my  "go to" shoe. Some really under appreciated thought and technology went into this shoe. I'm betting the Kaynao 16 is also stellar and if the price keeps dropping I could be lured into getting a pair as well. Looks like ASICS is really focused in on their running shoes and the Kayano could well be the cream of the crop. Hey like I said I was not an ASICS fan and now I will likely buy nothing else unless another brand has a crazy sale on a top of the line shoe. I'll will post a review of the Nimbus after I've worn them awhile. This now makes this my 3rd ASICS purchase in the last month. That speaks volumes as to what I think of this shoe company. Buy them while you still can. This will be and already is a highly sought after shoe.

review: I want to write this review to assist others when purchasing boots online as I learn so much from reviews myself.  I like Clarks shoes so thought I'd give these boots a try even though other reviewers had problems with the fit.  I usually like flat shoes and although these boots have a bit of a heel, they are very comfortable.  The leather and style are superb.  I have a somewhat long and narrow foot and usually wear a 9.5N if I can find the narrow size.  I bought a 9.5 M and the foot part fits well although slightly long.  I had no trouble zipping the boot over my straight legged jeans.  My naked calf measures around 15" at the largest part.  The problem for me is that the ankle area is too large so the leather is bulky at the ankles.  These boots would be outstanding if it weren't for the ankle fit.  They may be a nice fit for others, but not for me.  I like them so much that I'm contemplating sending back the 9.5 and ordering a 9 in hopes the shorter boot might reduce the ankle bulk.  The medium width will not work for those with a wide foot.  Hope this helps.

review: This is a fun, moderately heavy men's watch.  The band and casing are in a manly stainless steel, while the face and hands and stopwatch dials have a polished silver appearance that has a surprising amount of glitter.Description: This is good looking, with a moderate heft to it. It's heavy enough that you know when you're wearing it, and the big stainless steel housing is eye-catching. The face is almost rhinestone level glittery, with a silver background and extra shiny silver accents. The watch is water resistant, and has a light glow in the dark for after hours time telling. The three small dials together have a stopwatch (chronograph) function, which is cool and tracks seconds, minutes hours. With all of the watch links intact, the watch is about 127 grams (4 3/8 ounces) and is about 21 centimeters (just under 8 1/2 inches) in interior circumference when the clasp is closed. Invicta has a ton of styles, so if a silver face, stainless steel body, and eye catching glitter doesn't do it for you, I'm sure they have something that will.Resizing: One slight gripe is that the manual doesn't give any information on resizing. For those of you wondering how do to it, here is is:1) If you need to shorten the watch a centimeter or less, you can make small adjustments pretty easily with the "micro adjustment holes." If you look at the back of the clasp, you will see four holes in a row. You can use a pushpin to push the spring bar loose (be careful not to let it pop out and get lost!) and reset the pin in one of the closer holes without too much trouble.2) If the watch is more than a centimeter too big, then you have to remove some links. If you look at the back of the band, you will see that the last four links on each side have thin pins holding them to the next link. Check the internet if you're going to do a DIY resize , but basically, first you remove the clasp, then you take out one or more pins, either (very carefully) with a hammer and a pushpin, or more precisely with something like the Invicta Watch Sizing Tool, remove some links, then put a pin back in to attach the remaining links. Save your links in case you ever need to pass the watch on to someone with bigger wrists. Be careful not to bend or lose the spring bars or pins. Alternately, take the easier path and take this to a watch store to get resized.
summary: Heavy Steel Watch with a LOT of Dazzle Factor

review: The outside dial seems to be simply glued to the faceplate and it fell off after a couple of months, I tried to put it back in place but that did not last long either so I left the watch without it. Eventually, the glass shattered on the top left side when I accidentally hit the watch on the side of my desk as I was moving. Tiny bits of glass got stuck under the minute and hour hands rendering them unable to move and ruining the watch. I can't help but wonder whether the watch would have sustained the shock without the glass breaking had the top dial been in place.Besides that, during the ten months or so that I owned the clock, it felt of average quality, slightly heavy to be worn continuously as well, it never felt cheap though and looked good. It kept time correctly and did not drift extensively in any manner that I could notice. The stainless steel frame was good and did not stain though scratches eventually became some evident. Finally, while the hands seem to be able to glow, they do so very faintly and barely retain the glow effect for a brief moment.

review: A self winding movement relies on a half circle of metal that revolves around a central axis to periodically wind the main spring that keeps the watch ticking, in this particular seiko model there is a tendency for the tightening nut on the weight to come loose, whether by design defect or poor workmanship I am not qualified to say, but it is a regular problem with these watches, within months to a few years the weight will loosen until it can no longer revolve and wind the watch.When this happened to mine, I carefully unscrewed the back with a watch tool wrench,( you may also use fine nose pliers through a cloth) and being careful to not dislodge the rubber gasket, carefully re-tighten the copper colored metal nut over the weight's axis. Carefully reseal your watch, do not dislodge the rubber gasket.This seems to be the only major defect in this watch, and only affects a small percentage within the first 5 or so years, though I believe it will affect close to 100% of these watches within a decade of regular use. The big issue is service. Even if you can get a watch repair facility ( very tough nowadays) the cost of this simple repair will set you back over a hundred bucks.In all other regards this is a beautiful watch, but it is an mechanical watch, inherently inaccurate, always the potential for failure due to a myriad of moving parts. The buyer of a mechanical watch is buying an modern antique, and must understand the limitations of old tyme technology: you will be resetting the time every couple of days for accuracy, you will not subject the watch to excessive shock due to impact, and so on.I bought this watch because my first watch was an automatic seiko, and I wore a automatic seiko diver through the mid seventies into the nineties with no problems( a gift from my dad)with many great memories, I did not buy this watch for its accuracy, toughness or durability compared to modern eco-drives or quartz watches,( I have a great citizen for actually diving and ocean swimming)but because I like having a piece of 'high tech' from when I was young, pure nostalgia.If you don't mind learning a little basic watch maintenance and repair, and appreciate the skull sweat that went into making a collection of gears and spring into an reasonably accurate time piece, and love a really big, heavy, manly watch, this is for you. Otherwise stick to the ecozilla or other quartz divers and you'll be happier.

review: This is an awesome watch.  Great dive watch, great automatic watch (winds from the movement of your wrist), great BIG watch.With a stainless steel bracelet or a rubber strap, this is water resistant to 200m, with strongly luminescent markings on an orange dial, and a unidirectional bezel.  The orange was selected for visibility at depth, and the bezel that rotates in one direction is a hold over from the days before digital dive computers.  The large dial also aids visibility.It uses an excellent and proven movement from Seiko, the 7S26, the basic movement of the Seiko 5 series of watches.  "5" for the five traits; 1. Automatic, 2. Water resistant, 3. Shock resistant, 4. Date, 5. Day.  For someone who wants an automatic without paying Swiss prices (or wants to avoid Invicta and their issues), this is an excellent choice, as it would be for someone buying their first automatic watch.  No kidding, this movement keeps time as well as the workhorse of Swiss movements, the ETA 2824-2.This watch is a big watch.  We're talking one serious hunk of steel. About 43mm diameter and 11mm thick, this is pretty darn big, and carved out of a block of steel, so heavy also.  This might put some folks off, and some folks might go for it for compensations' sake, but I like a watch like this for outdoors, working out, and other tasks that would put a lesser timepiece in mortal peril.I'm sold, this thing rocks.E.M. Van Court
summary: Accept no subtitute, go for the new icon, the Orange Monster

review: Everything about this pair of shoes says cheap, cheap, cheap. I wish I had paid more attention to other reviews. Save your money and buy a pair of theirSkechers Women's Verified Fashion Sneaker, which are far more comfortable and much better quality.I am a true size 9 but unfortunately the sizing of these shoes is a bit off; my toes hit the tip and the toe area was much too wide. The shoes are very lightweight, with a soft insole and little support on the sides, which gives the feeling more of a slipper than a true shoe. After trying them on for a bit my feet hurt.As mentioned, the quality is also inferior to other Sketcher shoes I have. The three-part foam and rubber soles that provide great traction and comfort on many of their models is replaced by a one piece molded lightweight synthetic with what looks like a thin, fine-gauge fabric imbedded along the middle. I've only seen this on children's water shoes and with use it tends to wear away. Shiny gray paint on the side of the outer sole provides some interest along the side but also raises questions of whether it will peel or not with time.The sides and top are covered in polyester type fabric with a herringbone pattern which matches my tablecloth and napkins. An overlay of a thicker rubber-like matte fabric helps tone it down a bit. The inside is lined with soft padded mesh. There is not much support in the heel area and little padding around the ankle. In comparison, the Verified model has leather added to the sides which helps mold the shoe to your foot.The insole, nothing special - molded thin foam that's less than 1/8 inch thick throughout, with a little arch support (not enough to counter the lack of support on the sides), covered in a slippery, satiny material with a few holes for breathability. There's a huge difference with my other Sketcher's shoes, which have a lot more support, much thicker molded insoles, fabric mesh lining, and many more holes.The overall weight of this shoe is 6.35 oz; my other Skechers shoes all come in at 9-10 oz each. The difference in weight, probably the thinner materials used and the removal of much needed support foam.I love Skechers brand but this pair didn't look or feel as good as my others. They're going back.
summary: Too flimsy and lightweight - more like deluxe slippers

review: I happen to be a huge Kipling fan and have quite a few items in my growing Kipling collection. I needed a purse that I can use while traveling that held all the essentials.I don't know if it's just me, but it seems the "essentials" have grown in size. My medium sized wallet struggled to fit comfortably in the Alvar XS with my iPhone 5 (with an otterbox). There was little room left for a regular set of keys (car keys, mail, apartment, bike lock, keychain ring). If I happened to have a larger phone, like a Samsung Note 3 in a case, the Alvar XS could possibly hold this and not much else unless I had a money clip to hold cash and cards. With the advent of larger smart phones, make sure you measure out whether the Alvar XS can hold its weight.The length of the strap was disappointing. I like to have my cross body purses sit below the hip bone so I can comfortably reach into it. Take note from the reviews that this is a fairly popular bag with pre-teens. Despite this, I maxed out the length of the purse even with a 5'4" frame.Yes this is fine for the essentials. Chapstick, a smaller smart phone with a minimal case, and a very slim wallet. Anything more would be a very tight squeeze in this mini purse.

Nothing rubs against my feet causing any soreness, like new shoes can. They're my flats just like my Clarks Decade Capri heels that are so comfortable.Only thing is, the toe of these are a little more round than I'd like. If they were a bit pointier they'd be more stylish. But they are very comfortable. I do like the style otherwise.Edit to my review: They stretched and are too wide to actually fit properly. VERY disappointed because the Clarks heels also stretched and they are NOT cheap! I guess I need to go narrow. (What gives? I never used to have this problem. I know they're making clothes larger (for larger Americans?) but are they going there with shoes?!) Argh! How about a warning so that we don't waste our money on misfitting shoes!! They're bad for our feet!

review: We moved into a house with mostly wood floors, and in the winter, they get COLD and regular socks just slip like crazy--and with a baby that I carry around all the time, slipping is NOT an option. I've tried many different type of grippy socks and slippers, and haven't loved any of them--the gripper dots usually come off within one or two washings or trips around the house. So when someone recommended these yoga socks, I had to give them a try.Unfortunately, I don't really like them. The grippy knobs are nice--they've stayed on through a few washings already and they're small enough that you can't feel them when walking on them (a problem with some other grippy socks). But the actual toe part of the socks? Egads. It's hard to get your toes in the holes and takes some finessing (something I don't feel you should have to do with something as basic as socks). Then, once you have them on, the socks feel SO thick they are almost uncomfortable (I now know I can't stand to have things between my toes).As far as sizing, I wear a 10-11 and I ordered the Large and they fit just right. They do help with not slipping on the wood floors, but the irritation they cause when wearing them push them to the back of my drawer.
summary: I did not buy these for yoga - just slippery wood floors!

review: Before I say anything about the bag, you should do a search for videos on these bags and the different sizes they come in. I almost ordered the Large size, because it's just a few bucks more than this Medium one, and more room is always good, but that thing is a monster when you see it being used!As for the bag, this is really a perfect duffel in every aspect. I'm getting back into competitive fighting, and needed something to hold all my gear for sparring, as well as some pads to practice with when I see my friends who don't have their own. At first I was a little concerned that all of it might not fit, but once I got the bag in my hands, all worries were eradicated. The Medium is more than enough, and has TONS of room! The main storage compartment holds my gloves, an Everlast Thai pad, shorts, shin guards and much more. I'm sure I could throw another Thai pad in there and everything would be just fine. Also inside this area is a small pouch, and would be very handy to put your mouth guard in, or maybe even money, a wallet, phone or mp3 player.The two pockets on the front and back are ventilated, and perfect for storing any workout clothes you just got done with that need a bit of airing out. One of these pockets has a handle at the bottom (visible in the main stock image above) as well as a custom 88 card if you're part of a team. Just black out the white segments to make your number and you're set. If you're not on a team sport, just flip the card over and you have a blank card to write down your contact info on in the off chance that you lose this thing.The last pocket is on the side, under the Under Armour letters, and this pocket has additional pouches inside for even -more- storage! Supplements, snacks, whatever you need to put in here, go nuts. I currently have my grip strengtheners and supplements in there, and there's more than enough room for more stuff.And then you have the standard adjustable shoulder strap with a pad that can be moved around. The top of the bag also has two handles that can be held together with a velcro closure. Really, the only thing I'm slightly worried about with this bag is that the hooks that hold the strap to the bag are made of plastic, and it might be hard to replace these if they break.Again, look for videos that show the different sizes if no stores in your area carry these bags so you know what size is right for you! I'm very happy with my purchase, and I love the red/white color.
summary: I'm sure you can fit a kitchen sink in here if you wanted to.

review: Having plantar fasciitis, I need a supportive sole even to putter around the house. Orthaheel slippers worked well for me. Sadly, Orthaheel build quality is not what it was; my last pair didn't hold up long with only light indoor use. So rather than purchase another pair, I looked at other brands. I decided to give these sandals a try based on Dunham's reputation and the reviews others posted.They are well-made: reinforced seams, solid foot-bed, quality materials. I especially liked the outsole, suitable for carpeted and wood floors (the only place my sandals see) but with treads to handle outdoor surfaces, too. Unfortunately for me, the sandal is made for a lower instep (i.e., flatter foot) and I have a higher instep. The sandal vamp was too tight; I couldn't insert my foot fully into the sandal without jamming and wiggling it. I also found the insole insufficiently supportive.More's the pity...I really hoped these would work for me.

I actually am usually warm - and less likely to get cold. In other words I'm not the woman in the office shivering in the AC or going outside with a jacket at 55 degrees. I grew up in MN so I'm well used to cold (though the 25 year layover in SoCal seems to have thinned my blood a bit!)These are comfortable and fit nicely. I am 5'1" and 130 or so and bought a small. The leg openings are very small. I wear a 6.5 and I can barely get them on so I would be concerned for someone who is taller with larger feet.In the end I bought a cheaper (much!) pair of Champion Premium compression leggings that keep me warm even into the teens and that's what I always reach for to run. review: I've tried on several trail "barefoot" shoes and these feel awful.  I normally wear a 7.5B (normal width) and most of the NB shoes in 7.5 are plenty wide enough not to cause any friction points.  These feel like they would shear the skin from the bone if I were to try to run in them.  The strap across the instep presses hard against the base of the big toe bone (where a bunion would be if the toes aren't straight).  It's tight, narrow, and inflexible; not a barefoot feel at all. If your feet are extra narrow, this might work out for you, but the shoe's upper must be more flexible for my needs.The length runs about a half size too short compared to most shoes, and it feels a whole size smaller than the NB V0 Minimus Trail shoe.  The width of the V0 version is like a wide, and this V10 is like a narrow.On the positive side, the sole is very flexible and yet protective enough that you won't bruise your tendons on rocks or tree roots while trail running.
summary: Too restrictive for real barefoot feel

review: When I first ordered this product it came in a box with no padding and not in a bag, needless to say the container was cracked and leaking. This was the fault of amazon (or at least I thought at the time) who kindly refunded me as they always have exceptional service. I decided to try and reorder it and this time it came nicely padded and not broken but the problem does not end here.After having this cleaner for not very long it is very easy to see why the container broke in shipping, it is absolutely terribly made and I cannot stress that enough. The plastic of both the container and the lid are cheap and very thin. ANY cleaner that is mildly carcinogenic should have a thick and sturdy container with a sturdy lid that has a GELLED seal in order to create the tightest seal. All liquid cleaners I have ever purchased have this except this one (most liquid cleaners are slightly carcinogenic). The foil seal that covers the opening that is removed before using came right off when I just unscrewed the lid for the first time, no seal whatsoever, terrible.After having this cleaner for not too long I traveled somewhere and carried this along with other liquid cleaners with me (I am a jeweler). They were all in separate plastic bags and wrapped individually in bubble wrap to protect them well in my luggage. None of the cleaners broke, except this one. I can't believe the company would produce a chemical liquid in a container this cheaply made. For this reason alone I do not recommend.If you insist on buying this cleaner you absolutely must immediately transfer it to a new leak-proof container. That is if it has not leaked everywhere in your package already.On to the actually cleaner:The cleaner is nothing special. I understand it is desirable to drop jewelry into a liquid and rinse it off to get it clean as it requires no effort, but I can assure you you won't get the results you wish. First off never use this cleaner without gloves on your hands. The directions say to submerse it in the liquid for 2 minutes but I can assure you it takes longer than that. Place silver jewelry one at a time in the liquid for 5 minutes. Remove and rinse thoroughly with hot water for several minutes. Gently pat dry with a paper towel. Place back in the cleaner for another five minutes. Remove and rinse thoroughly again with hot water for several minutes. Depending on how tarnished your jewelry is do this 2-3 times but no more than 3 times. Do NOT leave it in overnight, you should never do that with a liquid cleaner. After you are done wipe down with a basic polishing cloth. I used this for silver chains and only chains because chains trap dirt and oil and trap any other kind of non-liquid cleaner.My advice is to pass on this polish. Check outBlueMagic 400 Metal Polish Cream - 7 oz., I can't recommend it enough. It works on ALL metals, including silver. It leaves them looking shiny and better than new and really puts a seal on it to protect the jewelry. Yes that is a cream which means it requires some elbow grease to scrub the jewelry and may take a while but I can assure you it is worth it. You will be mind blown with the finished product and it will last much longer. Trust me on this. Avoid this product.

review: I wear a 38H and ordered (and returned) several nursing bras, trying to find a comfortable fit. I wish I had returned this bra, but I needed something to take to the hospital with me. Because there is no under-wire, there is no "separation" of breasts, so at my size they are just floating across like in a sports bra. However, a sports bra isn't v-cut, or low-cut, like this bra is, so a sports bra at least keeps the girls contained. In this bra, if I bend forward (which is frequently with a newborn?), my breasts spill out, causing that awful quadruple-boob effect, where the top half of each breast is breaking free from the bra. It's a major annoyance to have to rearrange all the time. It's not even a good bra for sleeping if you sleep with nursing pads in, as the v-cut allows one breast to nearly fall out if you're on your side, and your bra, shirt, and bedding will be wet because the nursing pad won't line up with a breast that isn't contained in the bra. I kept this bra for the days I have to wash my other bra, but I hate wearing it. This has been a very hot summer too, and when I wear this bra my breasts are pressed up against each other. Between the heat and the hormones, I sweat a lot, and with no place for that sweat to go, I have a rash that developed between my breasts. Wearing a true under-wire that separates the breasts will not cause such a problem. I do not recommend this bra. Had much better success with an elomi bra.
