review: One of the great things about deck shoes is that, one expects, they are immediately wearable.  That is to say that they can be comfortably worn for long periods almost immediately.I've previously owned about a dozen pairs of them (Bass, Dexter, Sperry and one pair of Brand X) all of which were, as I said, almost immediately comfortable.This pair of shoes is different which I put down to two reasons, stiff upper and hard sole but let's do the bad stuff later; first the good stuff.1) These shoes look reasonably well made.2) These shoes are not entirely unattractive.Yep, that's about it.  They don't look awful and they look pretty well made.  I like a different toe shape and they are Made in the Dominican Republic (at least it's not China) so they probably aren't as well made as they could be if they were Made In USA.The bad stuff is that the upper part of the right shoe near where the tongue meets the body immediately dug in to my foot and caused me to take them off after only about an hour.  The next time I wore them this issue was not nearly so pronounced but after standing in them constantly for a couple of hours the soles of my feet were actually sore from the stiff, unforgiving soles.  My third time wearing them was less uncomfortable but I was still quite surprised at how uncomfortable they remain and that it is taking so long for these shoes to break in.  It is possible that this means that they are going to last a long time but I have to say that my Bass deck shoes lasted for a couple of years and were immediately comfortable (which is probably why these Made in USA shoes were taken out of production, they were just too good).  I'll be surprised if these turn out to be as durable, and greatly disappointed if they remain uncomfortable.I'll check in with a comment in a few months, perhaps.
summary: At first blush, these are surprisingly uncomfortable.

review: I ranked these pants as fitting as expected only because the previous pair I bought were much too large. I am a size 42 waist. The size 40 in Dockers was too large, so I kept buying smaller sizes. The 38-inch fits perfectly. Men's clothing has apparently gone the way of women's clothing trying to make us fat Americans think we're not so fat. My 10-year old, 42-inch waist pants fit me perfectly--my waist measures almost exactly 42 inches. Now, with the new "slim down America" style, 38-inch waist fits me perfectly. The only thing that changed was the size the manufacturer labeled the product.  Same thing has happened in women's sizes. A size 8 dress is now a size 4. So, beware of this trend when you order your size. Once sized correctly, the pants look good, and after one washing (so far) they retained their crease well. There is no label on the pants to indicate machine washable, but on Amazon's description, they indicated it was machine washable, and it is.
summary: Make Sure the Size is Correct

review: The Lowepro Messenger 200AW camera bag is incredibly rugged, good looking, heavy camera bag. For my uses, I prefer Kata's approach.This is an excellent camera bag made of outstanding materials with heavy-duty construction. The exterior is like the old military canvas material that will wear like iron. It looks great and feels wonderful in my hand. I can see this would take years of being ridden hard and put away wet. There is padding all around, equipment is coddled in thick protective insulation on all sides including top and bottom. I like the built in handle with a small padded grip on it. Those are so handy when I want to move my bag, but don't want to put it over my shoulder. Once my equipment is safely inside, I can kick this bag around and be certain my equipment is safe. I love the look of this bag. Messenger bags are so darn cool and this one is right there in the cool zone, not looking like a camera bag. The color says slate gray, but it is more like a dark olive drab - a great color.Where the bag and I differ is my equipment and a direct comparison to a Kata bag. Lowepro's approach to bags is heavy material and thick padding. Make the bag heavy and the gear will survive. Kata, on the other hand, has taken the approach of lighter materials, and lightweight foam padding in critical places. An equal volume Kata bag will weigh about half the weight of a Lowepro. I am mostly a sports photographer, and I carry a Canon 7D, a 70-200mm f2.8, and a 24-70mm f2.8 lenses. The critical factor for me, can I get the 7D with the telephoto attached inside the bag, preferably with the lens shade attached in the ready to use position (this makes for a really long camera that is wide and heavy)?It is possible to rearrange things for this bag to hold that pig of a lens and camera; it has to lie on its side using up almost 3/4 of the bottom of the bag. That does leave room for the wide-angle zoom in a corner compartment. I can ditch my video camera in the front pocket. I'm not a huge fan of this arrangement. There is a lot of wasted space inside the bag. Placing the lenses all in vertically works great, except I have to take the body off the telephoto. The body and telephoto together really doesn't work in the vertical direction - there's very little protection for the body at the top. For somebody that uses smaller cameras and lenses, this bag will work perfectly.If I was looking for a bag to hold a TON of gear (this thing will suck up the equipment) and would set up on location without looking for fast changes - this is the perfect bag. It is perfection for the location photographer. From what I can see this darn bag will hold two bodies in the front pocket (very well designed pocket for bodies), and three or four lenses on the inside along with a flash. But this isn't an arrangement I would hold on my shoulder and try to work out of the bag. There are just too many moving parts.  A wedding photographer would love this bag.There is one design flaw that I hate - the rain cover. I have an older Lowepro bag with a rain cover, that thing is super easy to put on and it works perfectly. This cover is a disaster. It took me about an hour to figure out how to finally put it on, and trust me it was tricky and I'm pretty darn handy. There is no way I could pull this up over the bag in the event of a sudden downpour. Never in a million years would that thing go on quickly enough (I can't even begin to explain how to use the thing).I love the brilliant zippered pocket at the rear. There is a zipper at the top that works for slipping stuff in if you want to use it as a pocket. There is also a zipper at the bottom that allows you to slip the bag over the handle on a roller suitcase - super safe and secure on a suitcase.The front flap is incredible. The card that comes with the bag shows how to fold the flap around to either Velcro it down tight or use a magnet catch for silent opening.There are pockets all over the inside of this bag. You could spend days trying to find them all. The wide narrow one at the back of the main compartment is the most useful. Batteries, remotes, cards, all kinds of stuff can go in there. There are pouches at the two ends of the main compartment. I can't really figure out what to do with them. Anything that lands inside those would be lost forever.This is a fabulous bag for the right person. Camera bags are a matter of personal choice; I normally spend a long time trying them on before I pull the trigger on one. Some will love the heavy weight and all the compartments and space to store things. If you are a sports photographer or somebody that works out of your camera bag and has big lenses, you might want to look at different bag.Kata KT DL-GP-100 GearPack Micro Backpack for DSLR Cameras and Accessories

review: I got this on a promotion from the seller, so it only cost about $10. At that price, it was a good purchase. I really don't see that it is worth $20.So far, I do like the fabric. It is quite lightweight but seems to have strength to it. Admittedly, it has barely had any use so far.It is not big, but it is sized right for a "personal item" on a flight. It is convenient to have a small backpack that can be carried onto a plane and stowed under the seat.The zipper on the top seems ok so far. The snap closure for the outside pocket is of little use. Smaller items can easily fall out of that pocket.  I put some velcro on the bag to keep it closed a bit more tightly, and that helps. However, a zipper would have been much better, and, for $20, should have been included.For some people the pouch is a real plus. Personally, I found it to just get in the way when the backpack is being used. It just hangs there - if there had been another zipper, it could have done double duty as a separate compartment in the backpack. Since it was just in the way, I cut off the pouch. Now if folds smaller and there is no pouch to get in the way when you are placing items into or out of the backpack. I just fold the backpack into a square, or roll it, and close it off with a reusable cable tie. Definitely not pretty if pretty is a factor for you.I do like it - for a lightweight, smaller backpack, it has been dependable and practical. I like the ripstop vinyl.  However, I scored it lower because of the problem with the outside compartment and with the pouch. Just a word of caution, the seller does a masterful job of eliciting positive feedback about this product.

review: I will never learn.Koreans can't make Keds. "It's OK - but not for me. Back in 70s and 80s they were all i wore.The "70s* uppers were good, soft cotton material that moved with the foot and the soles were flexible. I tried to bend the soles of these prior to putting on and they are like they are cast in stone, Moreover the heels are so shallow no matter how tight I tie them, my heel slips up and out. I don't blame Korea or Keds, I blame evolution and my  narrow,"skeleton" foot.I went through life wearing size 6 Keds and  Ferragamo dress shoes in a  6AAA with a quad A heel. Gravity and natural elongating of metatarsal has lengthened foot to a 7. I foolishly thought a 7-1/2 might be more comfortable with a cushy sock.If we ever wanted to solve our unemployment problem, we might consider making what used to be American shoes, in America for American feet. review: I bought these shoes back in March and had very high hopes for them. I'm an 8.5-9 and first bought the 9; it was giant. I exchanged for an 8.5, which fit much better. Unfortunately, I have to agree with everyone who has commented that their feet get pushed forward while walking. These feel GREAT while standing-- but something with the elastic always shoves my feet forward when I'm actually, you know, walking. So the toe-box, which is decently roomy whilst standing, is made very uncomfortable with any movement. Also, while the elastic at the vamp feels okay out of the gate, after wearing these for an hour or so, it starts to cut into my feet badly. Wearing these while shopping one day-- so not exactly a marathon!-- left indent marks on the tops of my feet for hours afterward. Not blisters, but literal indents. I kept wearing these, hoping they'd break in... and they never did. Also, the suede is quite delicate, shows every little scuff mark-- and I had the denim color, not anything light. As much as it pained me given that these are fairly expensive for flats, I recently gave these away. They were just too darn uncomfortable. The discomfort, by the way, has NOTHING to do with the sole/them being barefoot shoes, since I have read some reviews complaining about the lack of support (they're supposed to be that way!)-- but rather with the elastic cutting in and pushing my feet uncomfortably forward. I give them a two and not a one because they're pretty cute, and are good looking for barefoot shoes. And if it were just me experiencing the weird 'pushing forward' issue, I'd say it's just my feet; but given how many comments I've seen on the problem, it clearly isn't-- and so I can't give them more than two stars.

review: I loved the basic design of this earring, but had some problems. Some parts of the silver was rough and lined, almost like finger prints. The amber: With this amber you expect the debris and possibly some air bubbles and/or air fractures. Since these are "heat treated", some of these bubbles and fractures would diminish or disappear as they are cutting and forming the piece of amber to the shape they desire. When the clear of the Amber and the debris are nearly devoid because of the mass number of air fractures, something is wrong. review: I ordered a pair of antique black women's Veronica back zip boots from Amazon on October 12, 2013 (received on October 15th and worn once or twice a week.)  On December 7th, one of the snaps broke on one of the back straps and is now hanging loose. This shouldn't happen with less than two months of wear!I emailed Frye directly, and (six days later) a customer service rep emailed me back and offered to mail me a couple of extra snaps for me to take to my local cobbler. For boots that are supposedly such good quality and at this price point---no, no, no...this is not the response I expected to receive.This company provides poor customer service, since now I must lose my own personal time to take my practically new boots to a cobbler, wait for them to be ready, pay for them to be fixed, and use my personal time again to drive and pick them up.I expected much better and was disappointed. Avoid this particular style with the snaps, and possibly avoid Frye altogether, is my advice. I'm pretty upset that I spent so much on boots that I thought would last for years, only to have them break after a few wears.As for fit, I'm normally a 9.5/10, and ordered size 10, which fits well. With an insert (an attempt at some arch support and a bit more comfort) they are too tight and not wearable for me. Without an insert, the sole is somewhat flat, hard, and not so comfy, but they fit well size-wise. But I liked the style (before I had a broken strap hanging down), and so was going to sacrifice a little comfort for that. The toe box is on the narrow side, and for that reason I'm glad I sized up.The antique black is not shiny at all like it looks in the photos, and not wrinkly like that. The leather is smooth and basically dull, no sheen. Not a problem, but just wanted to note that they don't really look like the picture. Possibly if you treated them with Sno-Seal or some other leather conditioner, they'd probably shine up.Overall, I wouldn't buy these again. For many people, I'd say these boots are an investment, and yet they are disappointing me in many ways.
summary: Pick another style without the snaps.

review: I was excited to find these.  They were priced right and they hit that sweet spot between an oxford and a tennis shoe that make them nice enough to wear at a (business casual) office, but not so dressy that I can't walk to work or wear them on the weekends.  The chocolate nubuck ones also exactly matched the color of the shoes I was replacing - brown, but a dusty looking sort of brown that goes with lots.  And all those positive reviews!  Of course I bought them.When I first put them on, they weren't exactly a delight on my feet, but they weren't uncomfortable either.  The soles are thick enough to provide some protection but not so thick that I can't feel what I'm walking on.  They weren't as springy as some walking shoes I've owned, but they offered at least a little bounce.  They weren't as airy as some shoes, but I didn't overheat in the first five minutes either.  (I guess the true test will come next summer, huh?)  They seem to be well-made, and like I said, they're attractive shoes with just the right level of casualness.So "good enough," I thought, and I wore them on my walk to work.  I was satisfied for a while...and then the pain crept up.  Not pain in my feet, though.  Or even in my back.  No, they gave me shin splints.  Both legs.  My feet aren't deformed and I'm not overweight.  I have normal arches and have never needed orthopedic shoes or insoles or anything before, so I almost didn't believe they were the shoes despite that being the obvious conclusion to draw.  All I can figure is that there's something subtle in them that makes me walk just a little...off.  I've never had a pair of shoes do that to me before.I bought a $10 pair of orthotic insoles and the relief was instantaneous.  So I'm keeping the shoes and I still like how they look; I'm just a little peeved at needing to buy something extra to be able to wear them without crippling myself.
summary: Not as comfortable as I had hoped they'd be

review: These shoes are amazing. Below is a detailed review:Look/Style:  They look fantastic!  They go with just about anything.  I wear them with jeans, board shorts, khaki shorts, swimming trunks, anything.  They have a very classic look and I get a lot of compliments about how they look.  They some with an extra set of laces so you don't have to keep the leather laces on them, but you can also go without laces because the tongue is connected to the shoe with a bit of elastic.Comfort:  This is where these shoes really stand out.  They have all the comfort of a Croc-like sandal without any of the downsides.  My feet get nasty sweaty in Crocs, but with these I am always comfortable.  The upper part of the shoe really help wick away moisture and keep your feet pretty dry.  They also breathe really well.  They are light, but protect your foot quite well.  There are no weird rubbing spots and I wore them out of the bag without any blisters or sore spots at all.  The innersole is a rubber material and quite comfortable.  Also they have an anti-bacterial treatment that really helps to keep your feet from smelling bad.  I wear them with and without socks.  They are comfortable either way.Durability:  I will post again after a few months, but so far, so good.  These things are holding up to the worst Africa has to throw at them.  There are no cracks or tears and the seams are all holding up very well.  I didn't expect them to last, but they are performing well beyond my expectations. I realize they're synthetic (which means they shouldn't cost as much as they did), but I had hoped the quality would be better since the material is so thick.

review: Way too much lace for my taste.  The straps and central part of the bodice are lace.  It is not stretch lace.  The straps are adjustable.  Because there is so much lace, your slinky clothing will 'catch' on it, since the lace, is not nylon and slippery.  The rest of the camisole is standard nylon slip material, nice enough for the job.  Its shirtsleeve lace hem falls to just the top of the hip, so is maybe 20" total, depending on how you've adjusted the straps.  The straps can extend that length, a good five inches, but then the bustline is quite low on someone of average proportions, like me.As the other reviewers reported, the bust portion of the camisole is oversized, and the waist portion is undersized.  The sizes themselves are confusing.  I wanted something very loose, so ordered both an XL and a 3XL, but in fact the bodice was nearly the same width, on both.  Only the bust portion was bigger on the latter.  I wouldn't call the bodice portion XL, but closer to an L.  Makes me wonder what 'model' they used to size the garments.The white and black camisoles I purchased will barely do the job sought, which was to provide extra shielding for semi-sheer tops.  Fortunately all that lace does not show through.  But I won't buy the full slip versions of these.  Got to find something more conventional.  If you like the lace idea, go for it.  But bear in mind, that anything slinky you have to wear OVER these items -- like rayon, silk, satin, viscose -- will 'stick' to the lace, on occasion.

review: Like a lot of women I've walked around in the wrong bra size for years (the problem typically is that we're wearing a band size too big and a cup size too small and yep, count me in) so I'm trying to find new bras. Hanes clothes, including bras always look kinda cheap to me with scratchy fabrics, unfinished seams, etc., but I'm trying to be a little more open-minded (I recently got Hanes slippers that I like fine). I tried on an Extra Small (no way) and then a Small at a local store. The Small fit around band-wise but the cups gave me "wrinkle boob", that is, the cup fabric wasn't smooth over my breasts but wrinkly especially in the middle, a look I hate and one which is common to thin fabric bras. But the kicker is at 5'8" I found the straps weren't long enough even at their fullest length. There was no way the bra was going to work for me. I'm not sure what the "comfort" or "flex fit" in the name are referring to because I found neither.

review: So when my brother told me about these, I got all shades of excited. I've dressed as a vampyre every Halloween for well over a decade now, and short of getting dental implants, I've tried everything else on the market. So, here's how I see these:The intrigue...-this is an ingenious idea that is long overdue!-they are entertaining once you get them fitted correctly-they are a "softer" plastic, so they should mold easily for many different sized mouths-very simply design, not too many things to break on themThe not so seductive side of them...-the mold that holds them in place isn't really secure to the fangs/plate at all (mine had been jostled out of place when I opened them).-the plastic is very cheap, those ones you get for free with the make-up kits are much more durable feeling-like mentioned in another review, they make large "lumps" through your lips, so it's very obvious something is there.So, all that said, I much prefer the "fangs in a coffin" style much more. They are much easier to mold and set, they are much more stable, and most importantly they look like teeth, not just cheap plastic. As the title of this review states, there is a lot of room for improvement, but this is a great start and I am glad I ordered them. The price of these really won't hurt your wallet to try them, so if you are even just curious to check them out, you may absolutely love them.
summary: Great idea, but there's definite room for improvement

review: First off, I don't have any foot or back pain; I was attracted to the idea of these shoes simply because of the fitness claims.Deciding I had better try something like this on before purchasing, I went to Kohl's to check out the selection. Of the fewer styles available in a store than online, I picked these hoping they would match more of my workout gear than the rest. I have to say, I immediately found them incredibly ugly. I still do.$100 later, I had the shoes home and was taking them for a test walk. I ignored the advice to use them for only 20 minutes at a time, max, at the beginning (this advice came to me on a handy little CD-ROM packaged with the shoes at the time; not sure if it still is). I wasn't in a bad way or anything, but I could definitely feel the burn ... or convinced myself I could.For the next few weeks, I kicked my stylish Pumas under the bed in favor of these clodhoppers, using every step as a chance to push my body's muscles and every standing-around conversation as an opportunity to work my core. I didn't really notice any difference in my body, either its appearance or its improved resistance to these shoes.Finally I lost interest in this and decided to resume my cardio workouts in shoes I felt good in, rather than convincing myself that it is a workout to run errands in these paddleboats of shoes. I really have to highlight yet again that these shoes are HIDEOUS. All shoes from this generation of Shape-Ups are. Just awful. Really, the only time you should buy something ugly is if it's, like, a pipe for your sink or something.I notice that now the newest Shape-Ups are from the "Liv" line. These are sleeker, much more stylish sneakers with the same muscle-working benefits and a shape that does not announce to the world that you like platform sneakers. I may look into a pair of those. I also own a pair of MBTs - the "original" brand to make shoes of this style. MBT actually makes dress shoes with the rounded-off heel, and while they are unusual, they aren't ugly and they're perfectly fit for those with health issues who might find these comfortable and want to wear them all the time. But before you buy a pair of fad workout shoes that double as hand weights - unless you need them for your back - be sure you actually WANT them. Oh well; at least I have a matching pair of bookends now.

review: This is the same review I wrote for black and white pin dot microfiber tie: I have come to appreciate microfiber ties. I'm no Tim Gunn, but I like to look nice in my work suits. This tie works for that purpose. It looks great. It ties and hangs well; and it is MUCH easier to clean than silk. Additionally, the first time I wore it I did get a compliment on how nice it looked. Now I know there are some traditionalists out there who won't be caught dead in a tie that isn't 100% silk. If you're that guy ... try this one. Just try it. If you look at it in the mirror and can honestly say you don't like it, then return it. Otherwise, welcome to the new world.

review: I had a pair of Naturalizers that looked just like this one, except the interior was a red color.  Anyway, THOSE shoes were amazing!  From the first day I put them on I loved them.  The leather was so soft and the cushion on was like walking on a cloud.  And I wore them A LOT!  Everyday, for miles I'd walk in those shoes.  But as time wore on so did the soles of my shoes.  I looked everywhere in hopes of finding them again.  When I saw these I thought I had found a replacement!  Not exactly.  The cushion in the shoe is not nearly as soft, and the sole is a little firmer too.  The toe box of the shoe is squared at the top and not as rounded as my first pair.  What's more, the leather of the shoe is not nearly as supple/soft so I am getting small irritations on my little toes.  I've been trying to break them in to get them to be as comfortable as their predecessor but I just do not see that happening.  So, while I will keep this pair, it is NOT what I was expecting.
summary: Not the same shoe I had before.
